content.full_text:
Granada Capital Company

Brigadier General Edward E. Munger


June 19, 1992

Mr. John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Subject:     Request for Determination of Hearing Participation
             Ball Ranch Project, Fresno County, California

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     I am a Fresno County Planning Commissioner and assumed this office on June 14, 1990. I spoke to you on March 19, 1992, with regard to a then pending general plan amendment hearing (Kesterson) and whether or not an economic interest I hold in a limited partnership option to purchase undeveloped land could constitute a conflict of interest in making a land use decision. At that time you suggested that I write you a letter on this matter and include all the facts relating to my interest. You further suggested that in the mean time that it might be best to abstain from the pending action. I followed your advice and am now responding to your suggestion that the matter be reduced to writing.

     This mater has now taken on some urgency as I have just been advised that another project, The Ball Ranch, will be presented before our commission on July 11, 1992. The Ball Ranch is a General Plan Amendment and Specific Plan proposal.


Request.     I am writing to seek two actions:

     1)   a determination from your office that I may or may not participate in the July 11, 1992, Ball Ranch hearing.

     2)   a clarification and guide line as to which future hearings and decision matters I may participate in which are located within the general area of the Ball Ranch Project and my limited partnership investment property.

Of the two actions, item one is of immediate concern; item two can follow at a later date.




5588 North Palm Avenue • Fresno, CA 93704-1913 • Tel. (209) 432-4905 • Fax. (209) 432-8571

Mr. John W. Wallace
Re: Determination of Participation
Ball Ranch Hearings, Fresno, California

June 19, 1992
Page 2

Background.

On November 2, 1990, my brother, Maynard Munger, and I invested a total of $20,000 as Tenants-in-Common to acquire two (2) units at $10,000.00 each in PALMIA, a California limited partnership, formed for the purpose of raising at least $500,000 to be used to secure an option to purchase property owned by Knox and Carol Blassingame. This purchase was made through an investment counselor, Mr. Alan Grass of Grass and Associates in Fresno, with whom we have done business and made investments over a number of years, and was first proposed to us on October 17, 1990.

As for this project, a total of $520,000 was raised by Mr. Graas. Of the total amount raised, $500,000 was placed in escrow as a non-refundable deposit to secure a two year option to purchase the property. Under the terms of the partnership agreement, at the end of the first year, the partnership was to make additional payments of $20,000 per month. At the end of the second year (1992), a balance of funds equalling 10% of the purpose price must be placed into escrow. To close escrow on the purchase and provide for other costs, the partnership plans to raise an additional $8-Million of equity. I have no involvement in the daily business operation of the partnership. Exhibit A is an extract of the General Description of Investment and Business Operation Plan from the Limited Partnership Agreement signed in 1990.

It is my understanding that the general partners are still working on raising the equity funds. If they do, the project will move forward. If they do not, then the limited partners, including my brother and I, will lose their investment. If an equity lender/investor is found, the limited partners have an election to be bought out or remain as a limited partner (see paragraph 2, page 8, of Exhibit A).

Neither my brother nor I, as limited partners, have any voice in this project nor have we or are we involved in the daily decision making process. I have no information as to the status of an equity investor other than the general partners are still attempting to locate a investor.

At present, my prorata interest is 1.923% ($10,000 ÷ $520,000). If the partnership is successful in securing the remaining $8-Million in equity, my interest will decrease to .117% ($10,000 ÷ $8,520,000). Please see Mr. Grass' letter of December 27, 1990, as included herewith as Exhibit B.

Blassingame Property Purchase Terms. PALMIA's contract of purchase provides for the following:

Total Acreage: 2,800
Total Purchase Price: $50,274,000
Cost per Acre: $18,000

Mr. John W. Wallace
Re: Determination of Participation
Ball Ranch Hearings, Fresno, California

June 19, 1992
Page 3

**Location of Ball Ranch in Proximity to PALMIA.**

I have enclosed a series of maps to assist you in your orientation and in making a determination as to the proximity of the respective properties.

**Exhibit C** is a generalized view of northeast Fresno. The rectangle in a solid line represents the area in which a number of developments have been proposed (Ball Ranch, PALMIA, Kesterson). The dashed line outlines the area of Northeast Fresno County which is under intense developmental pressure. It is also currently the subject of a review by the Fresno County Board of Supervisors to determine whether or not a regional plan should be considered.

**Exhibit D** is a map from the Ball Ranch Specific Plan onto which I have drawn in outline the West boundary of the PALMIA property. The distance along Friant Road between the north boundary of Ball Ranch and the point where PALMIA intersects with Friant Road is approximately ¼ mile. Both the Ball Ranch and PALMIA are located outside the Sphere of Influence of any community. Both properties are zoned Agriculture with various acreage sub-designations. It is my understanding, although I have not yet seen documents to support it, that the scope of the Ball Ranch project has been substantially reduced from the "project site" shown on this map.

**Exhibit E** is another look at the proximity.

**Exhibit F** are maps of each property in a larger scale. The "hash lines" on the north edge of the area marked "Sienna/Ball" are the revised scope of the Ball Ranch Project. This northern edge is the same point from which I made the distance measurements shown on **Exhibit D**.

**Exhibit G** is larger scale map of the PALMIA project.

**General Discussion.**

In general, there is a considerable topographic variance in the PALMIA project area East of Friant Road from that in the Ball Ranch area West of Friant Road. Where PALMIA extends south and appears to parallel Friant Road, there is a ridge line or hill between Friant Road and the PALMIA property. There is no line of sight at this point or access to Friant Road other than at the western tip of the project. As near as I can determine by scale, the PALMIA property, at its closest point "as the crow flies", is in excess of 400 feet and at its only access point to Friant Road, is nearly 3,000 feet (see **Exhibit D**). Although the plans suggest otherwise, there is only one point of access for PALMIA at Friant Road which is about 300 feet in frontage length.

Mr. John W. Wallace
Re: Determination of Participation
Ball Ranch Hearings, Fresno, California

June 19, 1992
Page 4

There are major environmental concerns which would apply equally to all property in this reason. Primary of these would be air quality, traffic, schools, and fire protection. There are also major differences between property located on the San Joaquin River side of Friant Road (West) as compared to property located in the area East of Friant Road. Land West of Friant Road is very environmentally sensitive not only because of its proximity to the San Joaquin River (and the desire of many in our community to establish a parkway), but also in the riparian habitat which now exists. Land to the East of Friant Road is, for the most part, hilly and extensively grazing land. Although there are also habitat concerns, the major problem to the East is the availability of water.

The principal difference between PALMIA and the Ball Ranch is the San Joaquin River frontage. The dividing line, Friant Road, separates two completely different sets of topographical, environment and developmental concerns and present completely different considerations as to present and future land use.

Other Facts.

I have reviewed several brochures available to me on the issue of conflict of interest, as well as your letter of December 2, 1991, to Mr. Darrell W. Larsen (your file I-91-460), which you graciously provided to me after our March 19, 1992, telephone conversation. I offer the following for your consideration.

On March 6, 1991, I wrote to Mr. Peter S. Cooper, a local MAI appraiser who specializes in this type of rural property. I asked Mr. Cooper three specific questions as they relate to the issue of an impact on economic value of my investment (see Exhibit H). Mr. Cooper's response of May 29, 1991, suggests that I abstain from participation as he could not offer an opinion without first doing an appraisal on the property although he agrees that any decision on the Ball Ranch, one way or the other, would have an equal impact on at least 25% of the surrounding properties (see Exhibit I).

I forward these documents to outline the steps I have taken, and the problems encountered, as I have attempted to make a determination as to an financial impact of my potential land use decision as a commissioner on the PALMIA investment. I have not been able to do so and it is obviously out of the question for me to finance an MAI appraisal on 2,800 acres for this purpose.

I have been told by Mr. Graas that the $18,000 per acre offer for the property by the general partners was at the high end of the value of land in the area in 1990. Given the economic conditions, he does not believe that there would be an effect on the fair market value as a result of this decision in the foreseeable future. I would encourage you to contact Mr. Graas directly.

Mr. John W. Wallace                                                                                     June 19, 1992
Re:   Determination of Participation                                                                            Page 5
       Ball Ranch Hearings, Fresno, California




       I am very supportive of the purpose and principles of the PFP Act. I have attempted, as best I
can on my own, to make a determination as to whether or not there is or could be a conflict in decision
participation as it relates to the Ball Ranch and Kesterson. I could not find, in my judgement, that such
a conflict exists. But, I want to be absolutely certain. I sincerely hope that you will rule that I can be
involved in the Ball Ranch decision.

       As for my second request, there is no doubt that in the coming months there will be a number
of General Plan Amendment requests for variances or other considerations for projects in the dashed line
area. This is the direction of growth for our county. I also am concerned that there may possibly be
an overall regional plan to consider as well as a San Joaquin River plan in this area. To what degree
can I participate in these actions? I would be very tempted to resign as a commissioner if I am unable
to serve my County in these processes because of this investment.

       I am certain you can appreciate that like many officials, I did not accept the position as a
planning commissioner with the idea that I could be put in a position that I would be unable to
participate in the process.

       I respectfully request that you review the enclosed documents and contact me should you wish
additional information or clarification.

       Thank you, Mr. Wallace, for your time and effort in this undertaking.

Very truly yours,


Edward E. Munger

enclosures.

sections.question_synthetic: Can a Fresno County Planning Commissioner participate in decisions regarding the Ball Ranch project and future hearings in the area, given his limited partnership investment interest in nearby PALMIA property, and if not, what future hearings in the region can he participate in?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a planning commissioner seeking FPPC advice, not an FPPC response letter. The document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections typical of FPPC advice letters. Contains extensive exhibits (A-I) and factual background. Document quality is degraded (OCR artifacts visible). Commissioner requests two determinations: (1) immediate - can he participate in Ball Ranch hearing on 7/11/92, and (2) future - which hearings in the general area can he participate in.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Can a Fresno County Planning Commissioner participate in decisions regarding the Ball Ranch project and future hearings in the area, given his limited partnership investment interest in nearby PALMIA property, and if not, what future hearings in the region can he participate in?

embedding.summary: Brigadier General Edward E. Munger, a Fresno County Planning Commissioner, requests FPPC determination on whether he can participate in the July 11, 1992 Ball Ranch hearing given his $10,000 limited partnership investment in PALMIA, a property located approximately 0.5+ miles away across Friant Road. He provides extensive documentation of the properties' geographic separation, topographic differences, and environmental distinctions to argue no conflict of interest exists.
